items.title: Iran Deal: $25B Asset Release + 60-Day Ceasefire + Staged Hormuz Reopening
items.idea: Trump declares deal complete: 60-day ceasefire extension, Hormuz reopens in stages, Iran dilutes HEU (no further enrichment, no nuclear expansion), US lifts naval blockade. In exchange, $25B in frozen Iranian assets released. Dark Side frames this as a first-step market deal — the hard problems (full nuclear resolution, Israel, Hezbollah) are deferred, not solved. Signing ceremony set for Switzerland June 19. The $25B is political lubricant letting Tehran claim sovereignty preserved.
items.angle: The verification premium replaces the war premium. Six days between announcement and Swiss signature is an active risk window. Any Iranian walk-back, Israeli escalation, or sequencing dispute puts the bid back in the barrel.
items.tweetA: Iran gets $25B frozen assets, Hormuz opens in stages, nuclear status quo maintained. Trump claims victory. Both sides get face. That is not peace — that is a 60-day time-out with a signing ceremony attached. $BRN $CL
items.tweetB: The war premium comes out of oil. What replaces it is not peace premium — it is verification premium. Did Iran sign? Did mines clear? Did Hormuz actually open? Did enrichment stop? That is the trade for the next 60 days.

items.title: Hormuz Is the Master Key: Oil to Inflation to Fed to Bonds to Equities to Dollar to Crypto
items.idea: If Hormuz reopens, the oil tax comes off the entire cross-asset dashboard simultaneously. Oil down implies inflation impulse cools, Fed hiking premium out of curve, bonds rally, Nasdaq inhales, dollar loses wartime bid, crypto launches. Gold at $4,300 is the control panel: if it holds even as the war premium fades, the market is pricing lower real yields and dollar liquidity — not just geopolitical relief. Dark Side called $4,300 gold in advance; it now acts as the level that tells you whether financial conditions are genuinely easing.
items.angle: This is the single biggest cross-asset inflection of 2026. Gold above $4,300 post-deal is a bullish signal for rates and dollar, not just a peace trade.
items.tweetA: Hormuz reopens = oil tax off, inflation cools, Fed backs away, Nasdaq inhales, dollar softens, crypto launches. One headline, every dashboard flips. The question is not whether to trade it — it is whether the verification timeline holds.
items.tweetB: Gold at $4,300 did not fade on the Iran news — it held. If gold holds as war premium exits, the market is pricing lower real yields and softer dollar gravity. That is a different and more durable trade than geopolitical relief. $GOLD $GLD

items.title: For Israel the Iran Deal Is Nearly Calamitous — Strengthened Enemy, Deteriorating Ally
items.idea: Dauber frames the Iran deal as devastating for Israel despite market celebration: a strengthened, more influential Iran with large missile and drone caches on one side, and a deteriorating US-Israel relationship on the other — with bipartisan calls to reduce or end US support accelerating from both parties. This structural shift in Middle East balance of power is not yet priced in equities.
items.angle: Most macro focus is on the oil and inflation dividend. The second-order effect: Israel's strategic position degrades materially. Watch Israeli defense spending and whether US-Israel security guarantees are revisited.
items.tweetA: Everyone is pricing the oil dividend from the Iran deal. Nobody is pricing what happens when Iran gets $25B, keeps its missiles, and the US simultaneously debates cutting Israel off. The Middle East just reshuffled — not in Israel's favor.
items.tweetB: Dauber on the Iran deal: the implications for Israel are nearly calamitous. Strengthened Iran. Deteriorating US-Israel relationship. Rising bipartisan calls to cut support. Two simultaneous threats. Not in equity prices.

items.title: Polymarket 85% Iran Deal by June 30 (From 42%); Kalshi 61% Normal Hormuz by Aug 1
items.idea: Polymarket jumped from 42% to 85% probability of a permanent US-Iran peace deal by June 30 in hours. Kalshi's Hormuz normal traffic by August 1 rose to 61% from 45%. The 24-point gap between the two contracts (85% deal vs 61% normal traffic) is the verification spread — the probability that a signed deal does not immediately translate to normal shipping operations due to mine clearance and timing.
items.angle: The verification spread is where the risk lives. 24 points of probability sit between deal announcement and actual normal Hormuz traffic. That gap narrows or widens based on what happens before June 19.
items.tweetA: Polymarket went from 42% to 85% on a permanent Iran deal in hours. Kalshi went from 45% to 61% on normal Hormuz traffic by August 1. The 24-point gap between those two numbers is the verification risk. Markets pricing announcement, not execution. $BRN $CL
items.tweetB: Iran peace probability: 85%. Normal Hormuz by August 1: only 61%. That gap implies significant chance of a signed deal that does not normalize shipping quickly. The trade is not the headline — it is the delta between deal and delivery.

items.title: Iran Deal Terms Mouth-Hanging-Open Bad — Six Days to Switzerland With Beirut Still Burning
items.idea: Dauber is openly skeptical: the terms floated are described as mouth-hanging-open bad and the devil is always in the details. A 6-day window between announcement and Swiss signature (June 19) is enormous time in a region where missiles, proxies, hardliners, and naval assets all have a vote. Trump himself called the Beirut strikes a mistake on a special day when the region was so close to a deal.
items.angle: The bull case is consensus. The bear case requires the deal to fall apart between announcement and signing. Six days is a long time in the Gulf — especially with Israel actively striking Lebanon the same weekend.
items.tweetA: Consensus: Iran deal is a win for markets. Dauber: the terms are mouth-hanging-open bad. One deal, two completely different interpretations. In 6 days either Trump gets his Switzerland ceremony or he gets a problem.
items.tweetB: The most live tripwire between now and June 19: Beirut. Trump called Israeli strikes a mistake on a special day. Israel struck anyway. Six days is not dead air — it is an active risk window with hardliners in Tehran watching.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom (Hartnett Analysis)
items.title: CPI Crosses 4% for First Time Since 2023 — Hartnett Maps S&P -4% Over 3 Months, -7% Over 6
items.idea: May CPI printed 4.2% — first time above 4% since 2023. Hartnett's historical framework: when CPI crosses 4%, the S&P is down approximately 4% over the next 3 months and 7% over 6 months. The 1994 analog is invoked: not an instant crash, but a bond-led tightening shock that trapped equities in a grinding range until leverage accidents surfaced. Iran deal removes one oil tail risk but does not fix PPI at 6%.
items.angle: The Iran deal is a relief valve for the geopolitical inflation tail. CPI at 4.2% with PPI at 6% is a different problem. Warsh's first FOMC is not a dovish event.
items.tweetA: CPI hit 4.2% — first above 4% since 2023. Hartnett: S&P -4% over 3 months, -7% over 6 months when CPI crosses that level historically. Iran deal took out the oil tail. It did not fix PPI at 6%. Warsh chairs Wednesday. $SPY $TLT
items.tweetB: The 1994 analog is not a crash story. It is stocks grinding in a range while bond volatility hammers carry trades and leverage accidents accumulate underneath. Strong flows, crowded positioning, rising yields all leaning on the same door. That is today.

items.source: Phil Dauber (citing Michael Green) / Phil Dauber Substack
items.title: Negative Skew + Surging Leveraged Long ETF Volumes — The Index Cannot Stop Buying
items.idea: Michael Green's Size Does Matter analysis: in a reversal of normal market behavior, skew has gone negative across many stocks — calls are cheaper than puts, the opposite of normal. This signals FOMO dominance over prudence. Volumes of leveraged long ETFs have surged to extreme levels. Passive index flows are mechanical and price-insensitive, creating a vortex that self-reinforces until it suddenly does not.
items.angle: Negative skew across many names simultaneously with leveraged long ETF surge is a classic late-cycle complacency signal. These setups do not end gracefully.
items.tweetA: Skew has gone negative across many stocks — calls cheaper than puts, the reverse of normal. Leveraged long ETF volumes surging. Michael Green: the index cannot stop buying. This is not conviction — it is FOMO on mechanical autopilot.
items.tweetB: When leveraged long ETF flows are maxed and skew has inverted, it is not because everyone is suddenly bullish — it is because everyone thinks everyone else will keep buying. That is a fragile equilibrium, not a durable bid.

charts.desc: Charts inside article — leveraged ETF volume data worth opening.

items.title: 6 Independent Valuation Frameworks All at Multi-Decade Highs: P/S 3.65x, P/B Above Dot-Com, CAPE 41.20x
items.idea: Six valuation frameworks — spanning revenue, assets, earnings, and smoothed data — all sit simultaneously at multi-decade extremes: P/S at 3.65x (more than double the pre-2020 peak), P/B above the dot-com bubble high, Shiller CAPE at 41.20x. Real EPS are at record highs but prices have risen faster. Only comparable period: late 1990s dot-com. The consistency across six independent methodologies strengthens the signal.
items.angle: When six independent methodologies converge on the same extreme, the noise is eliminated. Any further upside requires almost entirely earnings growth — no multiple expansion left.
items.tweetA: 6 independent valuation metrics all at multi-decade highs: P/S at 3.65x (more than 2x pre-2020 peak), P/B above dot-com, Shiller CAPE at 41.20x. Real EPS at record highs. Prices rose faster. The market is pricing perpetual margin expansion. $SPY
items.tweetB: The only comparable period to today's valuation picture: the late 1990s. Any further upside requires almost entirely earnings growth — there is no multiple expansion left in the tank.

charts.desc: Chart showing 6 valuation frameworks all at multi-decade highs simultaneously — worth opening article.

items.title: Goldman: $920B AI Capex in 2027 — Actual Could Hit $1.1-1.4T, Railroad-Era Overbuild Territory
items.idea: Goldman Sachs projects approximately $920B in hyperscaler AI infrastructure spend for 2027. Actual outlays could reach $1.1-1.4T as companies overspend — reminiscent of historical buildouts (railroads, automobiles). This supercharges revenue for picks-and-shovels providers even from elevated valuations. The critical gap: regular companies have not yet shown widespread quantifiable AI-driven profits.
items.angle: The railroad analogy is apt and sobering: the buildout was real, the rails existed, and almost everyone who financed them went bankrupt. Nvidia sells the picks. Who builds the profitable towns?
items.tweetA: Goldman projects $920B in AI capex for 2027. Actual could hit $1.4T. Railroad-era overbuild. The rails were real. The companies that built them went bankrupt. $NVDA sells the picks. Who builds the towns? $MSFT $GOOGL $META $AMZN
items.tweetB: $920B to $1.4T in AI capex 2027. Hyperscalers building massively. Regular companies not yet showing quantifiable AI profits. The gap between infrastructure investment and monetization is the actual risk in this AI trade.

charts.desc: Charts inside — AI capex projections vs. revenue monetization breakdown worth viewing.

items.title: SMIC N+3 Metal Pitch 32.5nm vs Intel 18A 36nm — China Achieving TSMC N6 Density Without EUV
items.idea: SemiAnalysis first STEEL lab report: SMIC N+3 in Huawei Kirin 9030 achieves minimum metal pitch of 32.5nm — 10% tighter than Intel 18A's 36nm. But achieved through aggressive DUV multi-patterning and DTCO, not EUV. N+3 reaches TSMC N6 density but pays in complexity, efficiency, and process control. The export control narrative that banning EUV would cripple China's semiconductor progress faces a new empirical challenge.
items.angle: The US bet banning EUV would halt China at 7nm. SMIC responded by stacking DUV passes until matching N6 density. Kirin 9030 is shipping today. The EUV export control theory needs revisiting.
items.tweetA: SMIC N+3 metal pitch: 32.5nm. Intel 18A: 36nm. SMIC is tighter. Using DUV multi-patterning, not EUV. China beats Intel is the headline. China approximates TSMC N6 by multiplying patterning steps is the story. Different implications entirely. $INTC $TSM $AMAT
items.tweetB: The US banned EUV exports to China. SMIC's answer: stack DUV passes until you match N6 density. More expensive, worse power efficiency — but it works. Huawei Kirin 9030 is shipping today. The EUV export control theory has a serious empirical problem.

charts.desc: STEEL lab teardown images inside — worth opening for process comparison visuals.

items.title: SemiAnalysis Launches STEEL Teardown Lab — Enters TechInsights PE-Owned Monopoly Territory
items.idea: SemiAnalysis publicly launches STEEL (Semiconductor Technology Equipment Engineering & Evaluation Lab) in Oregon, built over 1.5 years. Already generating revenue on advanced datacenter chip teardowns. The timing is described as inconvenient for TechInsights — PE-owned, currently being sold, virtual monopoly for decades with underinvestment in CAPEX. Competition in chip intelligence is now entering.
items.angle: TechInsights is the information monopoly everyone in semis relies on. PE ownership plus sale process plus CAPEX underinvestment equals vulnerability. SemiAnalysis entering changes who you trust for China chip intelligence.
items.tweetA: SemiAnalysis just opened a teardown lab in Oregon. They are competing directly with TechInsights — the PE-owned chip intelligence monopoly currently being sold. Competition in this space changes who you can trust for China chip intelligence.
items.tweetB: The chip intelligence industry has had one dominant player for decades: TechInsights, now PE-owned and for sale. SemiAnalysis entered with state-of-the-art equipment and published SMIC details TechInsights has not matched publicly. Information asymmetry in semis is shifting.

items.title: UK Royal Marines Seize Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker in English Channel — First Solo UK Interdiction
items.idea: UK Royal Marine commandos and NCA officers conducted a 6-hour operation to board and detain the Cameroon-flagged tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel — UK's first solo interdiction of a Russian shadow fleet vessel. Russia's crude exports were 3.46mbpd in May 2026 (wartime high) despite sanctions. Structural shift: London moved from denying port access to physical custody of a floating asset. Shadow fleet economics depend on ambiguity — seizure forces counterparties to reprice the risk.
items.angle: Happened the same weekend as Iran deal and largely buried. Physical enforcement of sanctions is categorically different from paperwork. Watch tanker insurance rates and shadow fleet operator behavior.
items.tweetA: While everyone watched the Iran deal, UK Royal Marines boarded a Russian shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel. First solo UK interdiction. Russia's crude exports at 3.46mbpd last month despite sanctions. The message: your ship could be next.
items.tweetB: Shadow fleet economics rely on ambiguity — weak flags, thin insurance, layered ownership. The UK just turned that ambiguity into a seized asset in the English Channel. A repeatable interdiction model is cost escalation for Russian oil logistics.

items.title: Warsh First FOMC Wednesday: SEP Dot Plot With CPI 4.2% and PPI 6% — No Cut Expected
items.idea: Kevin Warsh chairs his first FOMC meeting Wednesday with an updated SEP and dot plot — potentially the last dot plot as Warsh has questioned its utility. CPI at 4.2%, PPI at 6%. Iran deal removed oil supply-shock tail but PPI at 6% reflects pricing that will pass through regardless. No cut expected. Key data: May retail sales, industrial production, housing starts. BoE expected to hold at 3.75% (7-2 vote Thursday). G7 summit France Monday through Wednesday.
items.angle: Warsh's first dot plot sets the tone for the entire summer rates narrative. If dots shift hawkish reflecting 4.2% CPI, it could override the Iran deal relief for rates.
items.tweetA: Warsh chairs his first Fed meeting Wednesday. CPI at 4.2%, PPI at 6%. Iran deal reduced the oil tail — but PPI does not care about Hormuz. No cut expected. The dot plot could reset the entire summer rates narrative. $TLT $DXY
items.tweetB: PPI at 6%. CPI at 4.2%. The gap between producer and consumer prices means more inflation is still in the pipeline regardless of Hormuz. This FOMC is not a dovish event. Warsh's first press conference may be the week's highest-stakes moment.

charts.desc: BoA and JPM rate expectations cheat sheets inside full article.

items.source: Neil Sethi / Substack (Week Ahead Original Pre-Deal)
items.title: Pre-Deal Context: Iran Had 3 Competing Texts, Polymarket Was 42% — Scale of Position Unwind Revealed
items.idea: Written before the deal announcement: Iran was circulating at least 3 competing versions of a proposed interim agreement. All included similar elements (Hormuz reopening, sanctions relief, nuclear framework) but diverged on immediate versus future financial relief for Iran — a key concern for US hawks. Polymarket was 42% for a deal by June 30, Kalshi 45% for normal Hormuz by August 1. This context makes the subsequent jump to 85/61% an enormous positioning shock.
items.angle: The baseline expectation as of Sunday evening was only 42% deal probability. The surge to 85% represents one of the largest single-session political probability moves in recent memory — implying a massive forced unwind of short-oil and long-volatility positions.
items.tweetA: Context: Polymarket gave only 42% odds of an Iran deal by June 30 as of Sunday afternoon. Iran had three competing texts in circulation. In hours, that went to 85%. The magnitude of forced position unwind implied by that swing is significant. $BRN $GC $VIX
items.tweetB: Three competing Iranian texts on deal structure were circulating as late as Sunday afternoon. The core disagreement: how much financial relief Iran gets NOW vs. later. That question was not resolved — it was deferred to Switzerland on June 19.

items.title: Post-War Offshore System Under Threat: US Using Naval Power + Treasury + Dollar Rails as One Weapon
items.idea: Dark Side's meta-thesis: the Iran deal is not just about oil — it is about who controls the global market operating system. If Washington uses naval power, Treasury oversight, dollar rails, and market pressure simultaneously, the post-war offshore system has a problem much bigger than crude. The European banking complex, offshore capital networks, and financial elites that built careers on predictable policy channels are watching an administration willing to jam the global financial transmission.
items.angle: The Iran deal and the UK shadow fleet seizure are both manifestations of the same trend: physical enforcement of financial sanctions becoming real. The offshore system depends on predictable non-enforcement. That is changing.
items.tweetA: The Iran deal headline is about oil. The underlying story is about who controls financial plumbing. US naval power + Treasury + dollar rails used simultaneously = the old post-war offshore system does not just bend — it gets ripped out.
items.tweetB: European banks and offshore capital networks are watching a US administration willing to use military posture, sanctions, energy flows, dollar rails, and market pressure as a single integrated weapon. Predictability is oxygen for those networks.

items.title: SpaceX Raises $75B — Largest Listing Ever Hits Market Same Week as Iran Peace Deal
items.idea: SpaceX raised $75B in the largest IPO of all time and begins trading this week. Dark Side frames this as a liquidity flare — the biggest space asset on the planet hitting the market at the exact moment the Gulf risk premium leaks out of crude. SpaceX plus Iran deal equals the most perfect simultaneous bull catalyst setup in recent memory. The danger is this creates peak consensus positioning right before a verification event.
items.angle: Perfect setups are when risk builds underneath, not dissipates. SpaceX plus Iran equals everyone wanting to be long everything simultaneously. This is the setup that makes reversals violent.
items.tweetA: SpaceX raises $75B — largest listing ever. Hits the tape the same week Iran declares peace and Hormuz reopens. Lower oil, SpaceX halo, dollar softening, vol offered. The bulls have everything they could want. This is when you check if your entry price still makes sense.
items.tweetB: Largest IPO in history hitting a market that just got geopolitical relief. SpaceX plus Iran equals perfect conditions for maximum FOMO. Perfect setups are the ones where risk accumulates beneath the surface. The reversal when it comes will be fast.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Asia Analysis
items.title: BOJ Expected +25bps to 1.00% Tuesday — Ueda Hospitalized, Deputy Uchida at Press Conference
items.idea: Deutsche Bank economists expect a 25bps BoJ rate hike Tuesday to 1.00%. Governor Kazuo Ueda is hospitalized; Deputy Shinichi Uchida will handle the post-meeting press conference. Lower oil helps Japan as a massive energy importer, but a BoJ hike with hawkish Uchida commentary could trigger yen appreciation that turns disorderly. Dark Side frames the yen as the shock absorber for the entire Asia risk complex — if it squeezes too fast, the Iran deal relief rally can turn into a plumbing event.
items.angle: The Iran deal helps Japan via lower oil. The BOJ hike complicates via yen strength. Uchida's press conference tone is the under-watched event of the week.
items.tweetA: BOJ expected to hike to 1.00% Tuesday. But Ueda is hospitalized — Deputy Uchida handles the press conference. Iran deal gave Japan lower oil relief. A hawkish surprise from Uchida on Japan inflation could send yen somewhere very different. $USDJPY $NKY
items.tweetB: Japan: massive beneficiary of lower oil (energy importer). Risk: BOJ at 1.00% with governor hospitalized. The yen is the shock absorber for Asia risk. Uchida's press conference tone is the week's most under-watched catalyst.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Hong Kong IPO Analysis
items.title: HK IPOs: +45% Day 1, +67% at 3 Months — 60+ Listings in 2026, Edge Is In Filtering Not Basket Buying
items.idea: Hong Kong's IPO market has roared back with 60+ listings in 2026. Average post-listing returns: plus 45% day one, plus 49% after one month, plus 67% after three months. But dispersion is wide — not a buy-the-basket trade. Alpha concentrated in large-cap standalone listings with strong revenue growth, balanced cornerstone ownership, and names positioned for Southbound Stock Connect inclusion. Index inclusion and mainland China buying via Stock Connect are the second-wave mechanical demand catalysts. Lock-up expiry is the primary supply risk.
items.angle: The most underappreciated trade in Asia right now. Index inclusion is a mechanical demand event — passive benchmarks do not negotiate. Identifying the next Southbound Connect inclusion candidate before it happens is pure alpha.
items.tweetA: Hong Kong IPOs in 2026: plus 45% average day-one return. Plus 67% average 3-month return. 60+ new listings. But dispersion is extreme — you cannot buy the basket. The edge is finding the large-cap growth listing that gets Southbound Connect inclusion before the passive bid arrives. $KWEB $FXI
items.tweetB: Hong Kong IPO market is back. The real trade is not the first day — it is the index inclusion event. When mainland China money gets access through Stock Connect, passive demand creates a mechanical bid on top of already-strong returns.

charts.desc: Post-IPO return distribution data inside — worth opening for dispersion analysis.

items.source: Robert Sinn / Junior Mining Insider Report
items.title: Junior Mining Director Drops ~C$1M in One Day — Insider Surge Signals Sector Oversold
items.idea: Director Oliver Lennox-King at Nevada gold explorer Roxmore Resources (TSX:RM, OTC:GARLF) purchased nearly C$1M in stock in a single day, adding to an existing position of approximately 2M shares after also buying 80K shares at $4.20/share in May. Stock has been in a downtrend for months. Artemis Gold (TSX-V:ARTG) also seeing notable SEDI insider filings. Author launches monthly insider tracker for junior miners: surge in insider buying at distressed levels signals oversold territory.
items.angle: Junior gold miners beaten down while gold sits near $4,300 all-time high. When directors deploy personal capital at scale into a downtrend, it is a meaningful contrarian signal.
items.tweetA: Buried in junior mining: a director at Roxmore Resources dropped nearly C$1M in one day buying his own stock — on top of 2M shares already owned. Gold near $4,300. Juniors in a downtrend. Insiders buying aggressively. One of these is wrong. $GC $GARLF
items.tweetB: Gold at $4,300. Junior gold miners battered for months. Artemis Gold and Roxmore both seeing aggressive insider buying — actual cash into the open market. Junior miner insiders buying at scale with gold near ATH is a contrarian setup worth tracking. $ARTG

charts.desc: Chart of Roxmore Resources (RM) daily showing downtrend and insider buy level.

items.title: $SMH Posts Highest Weekly Close in History — Trend Follower Trimming at the ATH
items.idea: Semiconductor ETF $SMH closed at its highest weekly level in history. Cubic Analytics — a trend-following practitioner who caught most of the run — is simultaneously reducing exposure to select semiconductor positions. The message: process over prediction. Buy the trend, sell into the record, do not become the bag holder. Rotation signal, not a bearish call. But meaningful that a disciplined trend follower who profited from the move is stepping back at the all-time high.
items.angle: When the person who caught the move starts trimming at the record high, it is worth noting. SMH ATH plus AI capex at $1.4T projection plus extreme valuation backdrop equals a complex risk picture for semis.
items.tweetA: $SMH just hit the highest weekly close ever. A trend follower who caught most of the move is now trimming. That is not bearish — that is the game: buy the trend, sell the record. Do not confuse winning the trade with the trade still being on. $NVDA $AMD
items.tweetB: Semiconductor stocks at an all-time high weekly close. AI capex narrative has never been stronger. That is exactly when to check your position size. The people who got it right are stepping back quietly. $SMH $SOX

items.source: P Equity Research / P Equity Research Substack
items.title: Iran Conflict Ended After 107 Days: Crude -3.6%, SOX RSI at 60 — The Bull Case Laid Out Clean
items.idea: Iran conflict began February 28, 2026 and ended after exactly 107 days. Crude fell 3.6% to lowest since March 5 — nearly since the war began. PPI at 6%, CPI at 4.2%, SOX RSI at 60.67 on 1-year chart (room before overbought). Author thesis: Iran resolved plus rates held plus energy down plus inflation alleviated equals catalyst for market to go significantly higher. Semiconductors moved into oversold territory last week, set up for bounce.
items.angle: The clean bull case stated directly. The Hartnett/Dauber bear case is also real. SOX RSI at 60 with war risk off is a concrete technical setup.
items.tweetA: Iran conflict: 107 days. Crude -3.6%, lowest since the war started. PPI 6%, CPI 4.2% — a lot of that inflation was an Iran-war tax, not just monetary policy. If oil stays down, Warsh has cover to hold not hike. SOX at 60 RSI. That is the bull setup. $SOX $QQQ
items.tweetB: SOX at 60 RSI on the 1-year chart — plenty of room before overbought. Iran war over. Hormuz opening. Oil minus 3.6%. The bear case requires verification to fail. The bull case requires it to hold. $SOX $SMH $QQQ

items.title: Coinbase Allows AI Agents to Trade Autonomously — Autonomous Crypto Capital Allocation Begins
items.idea: Coinbase has begun allowing AI agents to trade directly on the platform — not humans using AI to decide, but agents executing independently. If this becomes a meaningful fraction of order flow, it changes crypto market microstructure: agent-driven order flow has different latency, different decision functions, and potentially correlated behavior across agents using similar models. Also noted: Anthropic had to disable Fable 5 following a US government action (details thin in the email).
items.angle: Coinbase enabling agent trading is quietly one of the most structurally significant crypto developments of 2026. The microstructure implications take time to manifest but the direction is clear.
items.tweetA: Coinbase now allows AI agents to trade autonomously — agents executing independently, not humans using AI to decide. If this scales, it changes crypto microstructure fundamentally. HFT firms spent 20 years optimizing for human-speed decisions. Agent-speed is different. $BTC $ETH $COIN
items.tweetB: Two AI notes this week: Coinbase allows agents to trade directly. Anthropic had to disable Fable 5 after a US government action. One is about the future of autonomous capital. The other is about the limits governments are drawing around it.

items.title: Recent Wins: ASTS +76%, FLY +94%, ORCL +56%, CIFR +51% — Technical Momentum in Semis and Tech
items.idea: SixSigmaCapital weekly trade ideas highlights recent wins: ASTS (74 to 130, +76%), FLY (32 to 62, +94%), NOK (12.4 to 16, +29%), RBRK (60 to 78, +30%), IREN (52 to 61, +17%), ORCL (160 to 250, +56%), CIFR (18.5 to 28, +51%), USAR (19 to 30, +58%). Entry methodology uses SMA 200 reclaims, IPO AVWAP re-tests, EMA 20 holds, and ATR-based stop-losses. Week of June 15 watchlist focuses on semiconductors and tech hardware.
items.angle: The win rate on technical setups in this momentum-driven market is notable. Post-Iran deal, momentum names in tech and semis could set up for another technical wave.
items.tweetA: Recent technical setups: ASTS +76%, FLY +94%, ORCL +56%, CIFR +51%, USAR +58%. Entries on SMA reclaims and AVWAP holds with defined stop losses. In a momentum market with clear direction, this is the playbook that works. $ASTS $ORCL
items.tweetB: ORCL went from 160 to 250. ASTS from 74 to 130. Not by predicting the top — by buying SMA 200 reclaims and EMA holds with defined stops. Process beats prediction in a momentum-driven market. June 15 watchlist: semiconductors and tech hardware.
